text: I’ll inspect the fixture app’s manifests and test setup first, then audit dependency versions against vulnerability data before editing anything. After that I’ll make the smallest dependency/code changes needed and run `make test`.
The repo is large and the fixture appears to be a Maven-based Java app. I’m narrowing this to the actual build manifests and existing dependency/version patterns now, so the remediation stays scoped.

tool: /bin/bash -lc "find fixtures -maxdepth 4 \\( -name package.json -o -name package-lock.json -o -name yarn.lock -o -name build.gradle -o -name gradle.lockfile -o -name requirements.txt -o -name go.mod \\) -print"
/bin/bash -lc 'command -v osv-scanner || true; command -v grype || true; command -v trivy || true; command -v mvn || true'

text: There’s no local OSV/Grype binary, but Trivy is installed and Maven is available. I’m going to use Trivy as the dependency vulnerability scanner, then cross-check any awkward cases against Maven resolution so we don’t only fix declared versions while leaving vulnerable transitive jars in the reactor.

The first scan found many repeated findings across module POMs, with `grouper-parent` carrying most managed versions. The table output is too noisy to trust manually, so I’m switching to JSON and deduplicating by package/version/fixed-version/actionable status.

tool: /bin/bash -lc "jq -r '[.Results[]? | select(.Vulnerabilities) | .Vulnerabilities[] | {pkg:.PkgName, installed:.InstalledVersion, fixed:(.FixedVersion // \"\"), status:(.Status // \"\"), sev:.Severity, id:.VulnerabilityID}] | group_by(.pkg+\"@\"+.installed)[] | {pkg:.[0].pkg, installed:.[0].installed, fixed_versions:([.[].fixed] | unique | join(\", \")), statuses:([.[].status] | unique | join(\",\")), severities:([.[].sev] | unique | join(\",\")), ids:([.[].id] | unique | join(\",\")), count:length} | [.pkg,.installed,.fixed_versions,.statuses,.severities,.count,.ids] | @tsv' /tmp/bigapp-trivy.json | sort"

/bin/bash -lc "python3 - <<'PY'
import json
from collections import defaultdict
j=json.load(open('/tmp/bigapp-trivy.json'))
g=defaultdict(list)
for r in j.get('Results',[]):
    for v in r.get('Vulnerabilities') or []:
        g[(v.get('PkgName'),v.get('InstalledVersion'))].append(v)
for (pkg,inst), vs in sorted(g.items()):
    fixed=sorted(set(v.get('FixedVersion') or '' for v in vs))
    statuses=sorted(set(v.get('Status') or '' for v in vs))
    sevs=sorted(set(v.get('Severity') or '' for v in vs))
    ids=sorted(set(v.get('VulnerabilityID') for v in vs))
    print('\\t'.join([pkg, inst or '', ', '.join(fixed), ','.join(statuses), ','.join(sevs), str(len(ids)), ','.join(ids)]))
PY"

text: The deduplicated scan shows two classes: packages with fixed releases we can usually force through parent dependency management, and older abandoned coordinates where the scanner reports no fixed version. I’m locating each package in the POMs now to separate direct version bumps from transitive overrides and true no-fixed-version cases.

tool: /bin/bash -lc "for p in 'not-yet-commons-ssl' 'jackson-core' 'jackson-databind' 'guava' 'google-oauth-client' 'protobuf-java' 'nimbus-jose-jwt' 'commons-collections' 'commons-httpclient' 'commons-lang' 'dom4j' 'vt-ldap' 'netty' 'json-lib' 'activemq' 'commons-compress' 'commons-lang3' 'apache-mime4j' 'neethi' 'xmlsec' 'velocity' 'jose4j' 'bcpkix' 'bcprov' 'hibernate-core' 'opensaml' 'esapi' 'pac4j-core' 'spring-' 'ion-java' 'xalan'; do printf '\\n## %s\\n' \""'$p"; rg -n "$p" fixtures/bigapp --glob '"'pom.xml'; done"

text: A few vulnerable packages are coming from transitive dependencies in modules outside the root reactor as well, such as the standalone `apacheds-ldappc-schema` and optional plugin POMs. I’m treating those as in scope because they are under `fixtures/bigapp` and Trivy reports them directly.

tool:
/bin/bash -lc "python3 - <<'PY'
import json, collections
j=json.load(open('/tmp/bigapp-trivy.json'))
for r in j.get('Results',[]):
    t=r.get('Target')
    for v in r.get('Vulnerabilities') or []:
        print(f\"{v.get('PkgName')}\\t{v.get('InstalledVersion')}\\t{v.get('Status')}\\t{v.get('FixedVersion') or ''}\\t{t}\")
PY > /tmp/bigapp-vulns.tsv
python3 - <<'PY'
from collections import defaultdict
d=defaultdict(set)
for line in open('/tmp/bigapp-vulns.tsv'):
    pkg, inst, status, fixed, target=line.rstrip('\\n').split('\\t')
    d[(pkg,inst)].add(target)
for (pkg,inst),targets in sorted(d.items()):
    print(f\"\\n{pkg}@{inst}\")
    for t in sorted(targets): print(f\"  {t}\")
PY"

text: I hit a shell heredoc typo while extracting the target map; no files were changed. I’m rerunning that extraction cleanly so each documented fix maps back to the affected POM(s).

text: I’m about to edit dependency versions only: central managed versions in `grouper-parent`, direct versions in the standalone/optional module POMs, and dependency-management overrides for vulnerable transitives that the scanner resolves through Maven. I’ll also add `FIXES.md` with one package entry each, including no-fixed-version cases.

Some fixed-version suggestions from the scanner are cross-major and may not exist for old coordinates, especially Bouncy Castle’s `jdk15on` artifacts. I’m verifying candidate versions with Maven before choosing edits, because a “fixed” version that Maven cannot resolve would just break the build.

I’ll bump older instances to `1.70` where possible and document the residual no-fixed-version status for the `jdk15on` coordinates.

text: I’m applying the version-only remediation now. Where the scanner points at abandoned coordinates with no Maven-resolvable fixed version, I’m leaving the dependency in place and documenting that explicitly instead of substituting incompatible APIs.
The first broad patch missed exact XML context, so I’m switching to smaller patches around stable anchors. That keeps the XML readable and avoids accidental structural edits.

The parent POM now carries managed overrides for the common vulnerable transitives and core library bumps. I’m adding the remaining web-service, SAML, Spring, and Netty overrides before touching module-local direct versions.

Next I’m updating module-local direct versions: ActiveMQ, Axis2, installer compression, jose4j, pac4j, test plugin commons-lang3, and the standalone ApacheDS schema POM. These are targeted version changes rather than refactors.

The generated WS client now inherits the upgraded Axis2 and Bouncy Castle versions from the parent. I’m doing the same for the main WS module and then I’ll validate the POMs before running the full build.

For the standalone ApacheDS schema POM, `commons-collections` can be overridden to the fixed `3.2.2`; its old `commons-lang` transitive has no fixed version on the `commons-lang:commons-lang` coordinate, so that will be documented rather than hidden.
The edited parent POM validates as XML/Maven. I’m rescanning with Trivy now to see which findings are actually gone and which residual ones are no-fixed-version abandoned coordinates.
The scan is still running; the Maven fixture has many POMs, so this pass takes about a minute. I’ll use the JSON result to adjust any missed direct versions rather than guessing from the old report.

tool:
/bin/bash -lc "python3 - <<'PY'
import json
from collections import defaultdict
j=json.load(open('/tmp/bigapp-trivy-final.json'))
g=defaultdict(set)
for r in j.get('Results',[]):
    for v in r.get('Vulnerabilities') or []:
        g[(v.get('PkgName'), v.get('InstalledVersion'), v.get('FixedVersion') or '', v.get('Status') or '')].add(v.get('VulnerabilityID'))
print(len(g), 'remaining package/version groups')
for (pkg, ver, fixed, status), ids in sorted(g.items()):
    print(f\"{pkg}@{ver} status={status} fixed={fixed or '<none>'} ids={','.join(sorted(ids))}\")
PY"
